text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Ahmed  
Maple Road Surgery  
12 Maple Road, Harrow, London HA3 7QT  

Dear Dr Ahmed,  

Sophie Bennett, DOB: 14-02-1993, Hospital No: N483920 NHS No. 9081726453  
22 Brookside Close, Harrow, London HA2 6LN 

Diagnosis: Generalised epilepsy 

Medication:  
• Lamotrigine 150 mg morning / 200 mg evening  
• Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily  

Thank you for reviewing this patient who has a confirmed history of generalised epilepsy. Over the past six months seizure control has been stable; she suffers one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every 3 months, with a maximum seizure-free period of four weeks. Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes. 

Despite the above stable numerical pattern, she reports a concerning qualitative deterioration. Specifically, her aura characteristics have changed with more rapid evolution into impaired awareness, and she notes a shorter prodrome and increased sensory distortion preceding events. The auras are now briefer and progress more quickly, particularly in the late afternoon and with sleep deprivation. She also reports greater post-ictal confusion and headache compared to prior baseline. There have been no injuries, but she has required assistance at work on two occasions due to prolonged recovery. 

She remains fully adherent to lamotrigine and levetiracetam and denies missed doses. No intercurrent illness or alcohol excess. Menses are regular and there is no clear catamenial pattern. She has been undertaking longer shifts, and sleep has been reduced to approximately 5–6 hours on several weekdays, which may be contributory. There is no new medication or supplement use. 

Examination in clinic today was non-focal and unchanged. Routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) from last month were within normal limits per patient report; we have requested copies for our records. She has not yet undergone the repeat EEG we previously suggested; we have re-booked this to evaluate for any evolution in interictal generalised spike–wave activity. 

Given the altered aura phenomenology with more rapid evolution, we regard this as a deterioration in her condition despite the apparent stability of event counts. We discussed lifestyle regularisation, seizure first aid, and maintaining a seizure diary with specific attention to aura onset, duration, and precipitating factors. She is fit to continue current duties with caution and agreed workplace adjustments around rest breaks. Driving status was reviewed in accordance with DVLA guidance; she understands her obligations to report and to cease driving if criteria are not met. 

Plan:  
• Continue lamotrigine 150 mg AM / 200 mg PM and levetiracetam 1000 mg BD unchanged for now while we reassess.  
• Arrange early EEG and consider ambulatory recording if routine study is non-diagnostic.  
• Repeat serum lamotrigine level for reference (non-urgent).  
• Follow-up in the neurology clinic in 8–10 weeks or sooner if escalation occurs (e.g., injuries, clustering, or prolonged post-ictal states).  

We will keep you updated following investigations. 

Yours sincerely

Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies mentioned: (1) "one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every 3 months" = 1 per 3 months. (2) "maximum seizure-free period of four weeks" is not a frequency but indicates they don't go beyond 4 weeks without seizures. (3) "Weekly absences persist" = 1 per week at least; could imply more than one per week but the literal statement 'weekly' supports 1 per week. (4) "atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months" = approximately 1 per few months, which is less frequent. Since multiple seizure types have different frequencies, apply the rule to select the highest frequency. The highest frequency is the weekly absences. Therefore final label: 1 per week. Quote the relevant line.
reference: Over the past six months seizure control has been stable; she suffers one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every 3 months, with a maximum seizure-free period of four weeks. Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes
